news_summary: Progress in human rights is marked by EU MEPs advocating for consent-based rape legislation, the Central African Republic advancing through landmark elections, and a coastal community successfully curbing harmful holiday rentals to protect local well-being.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès en matière de droits humains sont marqués par les députés européens qui plaident en faveur d'une législation sur le viol fondée sur le consentement, par la République centrafricaine qui progresse grâce à des élections historiques et par une communauté côtière qui a réussi à limiter les locations de vacances nuisibles afin de protéger le bien-être local.
news_summary_es: Los avances en materia de derechos humanos se caracterizan por la defensa de los eurodiputados de la UE de una legislación sobre violación basada en el consentimiento, el progreso de la República Centroafricana gracias a unas elecciones históricas y el éxito de una comunidad costera en la lucha contra los alquileres vacacionales perjudiciales para proteger el bienestar local.

articles.title: Press release - Time for EU legislation to define rape based on absence of consent, say MEPs
articles.summary: The Commission should, MEPs say, propose EU legislation establishing a common definition of rape based on the concept of freely given, informed and revocable consent.Committee on Women’s Rights and Gender EqualityCommittee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
The report, endorsed on Wednesday by the Committees on Civil Liberties and Women’s Rights with 75 votes for, 27 against and 3 abstentions, builds on Parliament’s long-standing position that the absence of consent must be the central element in court proceedings for rape.

An EU-level definition based on absence of consent

MEPs urge the Commission to table legislation establishing an EU-wide definition of rape grounded in the absence of freely given, informed and revocable consent. Member states that still rely on force- or violence-based definitions are asked to align their laws with international standards, including the Istanbul Convention, ratified by the EU in 2023, and to ensure adequate support and protection for victims and survivors.

The report stresses that only this kind of legislation can ensure effective access to justice, as evidence shows it improves reporting, conviction, and recovery rates. MEPs also argue that such legislation should complement the 2024 EU directive on combating violence against women and domestic violence, which introduces common standards on prevention, protection and victim support. They reiterate the call for gender-based violence to be added to the list of EU crimes under Article 83(1) TFEU.

A victim-centred, intersectional approach

Sexual violence is often compounded by other forms of discrimination, MEPs say, urging member states to guarantee comprehensive medical care, including sexual and reproductive health services such as abortion, psychological support and legal assistance. The report highlights the need for 24-hour crisis centres, free specialised services and effective reparation mechanisms. It also calls for extending limitation periods for rape offences, recognising that many victims report crimes only after significant delays due to trauma, fear or social pressure.

Tackling digital violence and harmful gender stereotypes

MEPs want EU guidelines in 2026 on comprehensive sexuality and relationship education, as well as for EU-wide awareness campaigns to combat myths around rape, and online misogynistic propaganda, including online anti-gender content and incel propaganda that normalise violence against women.

They call for tailored training for law enforcement, judges, prosecutors, healthcare professionals and frontline services to prevent secondary victimisation and eliminate harmful gender stereotypes. Enhanced cooperation with civil society, women’s specialist services and EU agencies, such as the European Institute for Gender Equality and the Fundamental Rights Agency,, is requested.

Quotes

The Civil Liberties Committee rapporteur Evin Incir (S&D, Sweden), commented: “It is unacceptable that in 2026, in parts of the EU, women are still not protected by consent-based rape laws. Today’s vote brings us one step closer to creating a common, EU-wide definition of rape. ‘Yes means yes’ — and it must carry the same meaning in every member state. Women and girls deserve equal protection, regardless of where they live. I now urge the entire Parliament to complete this work in March by backing this report, and I call on the Commission to put forward a legislative proposal.”

The Women’s Rights Committee rapporteur Joanna Scheuring-Wielgus (S&D, Poland) stated: “Much has changed since the Council opposed the inclusion of a consent-based definition of rape in the Violence Against Women Directive. Since then, the case of Gisèle Pelicot has shaken the whole world. Her courage to speak out, has opened the eyes of even the most conservative opponents of this change. We have an obligation to use this momentum, not tomorrow, but now. The European Parliament has listened to the voice of Gisèle Pelicot and millions of Europeans. The political will is there, so the only question that remains is: what is the European Commission waiting for?”

Next steps

The report is expected to be tabled at the March II plenary session (25 - 26 March) in Brussels.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the European Parliament's strong push for EU-wide legislation defining rape based on absence of consent, which represents a significant human rights advancement. This legislation aims to harmonize laws across member states, improve justice access, victim support, and address gender-based violence comprehensively. The scope is broad, affecting all EU citizens, and the article provides detailed context on the legislative process and intended impact.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament pushes for EU-wide rape law based on consent
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Currently, rape laws vary across European Union (EU) countries. Some focus on violence or force rather than whether the victim gave clear consent. This makes it harder for victims to get justice and support.

**What happened** – In March 2026, the European Parliament’s committees on Civil Liberties and Women’s Rights voted to ask the European Commission to create a law defining rape as happening when there is no freely given, informed, and revocable consent. The vote passed with 75 in favor, 27 against, and 3 abstentions. This new law would replace older rules based on force and violence.

**Impact** – Defining rape by the absence of consent is important because it focuses on the victim’s choice, not just physical force. This change is expected to help more victims report crimes, improve court decisions, and provide better support services like medical care and counseling. It also aims to fight harmful gender stereotypes and online violence against women.

**What’s next step** – The report will be presented to the full European Parliament in late March 2026 for approval. If passed, the European Commission will be urged to propose the new law. This could lead to all EU countries updating their rape laws to match this common, consent-based definition.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament is moving to create a clear, consent-based definition of rape across the EU, aiming to improve justice and support for victims everywhere.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen réclame une définition du viol basée sur le consentement dans toute l’UE
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Actuellement, les lois sur le viol diffèrent entre les pays de l’Union européenne (UE). Certaines se concentrent sur la violence ou la force plutôt que sur le consentement clair de la victime. Cela complique l’accès à la justice et au soutien pour les victimes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En mars 2026, les commissions du Parlement européen chargées des libertés civiles et des droits des femmes ont voté pour demander à la Commission européenne de créer une loi définissant le viol comme un acte sans consentement libre, éclairé et révocable. Le vote a réuni 75 voix pour, 27 contre et 3 abstentions. Cette nouvelle loi remplacerait les règles anciennes basées sur la violence.

**Impact** – Définir le viol par l’absence de consentement est important car cela met l’accent sur le choix de la victime, pas seulement sur la force physique. Ce changement devrait encourager plus de victimes à dénoncer les crimes, améliorer les décisions judiciaires et offrir de meilleurs services de soutien comme les soins médicaux et le conseil.

**Prochaine étape** – Le rapport sera présenté à la session plénière du Parlement européen fin mars 2026 pour approbation. S’il est adopté, la Commission européenne sera invitée à proposer cette nouvelle loi. Cela pourrait conduire tous les pays de l’UE à mettre à jour leurs lois sur le viol selon cette définition commune basée sur le consentement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Parlement européen avance vers une définition claire du viol basée sur le consentement dans toute l’UE pour améliorer la justice et le soutien aux victimes.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo impulsa una ley sobre violación basada en el consentimiento en toda la UE
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Actualmente, las leyes sobre violación varían entre los países de la Unión Europea (UE). Algunas se centran en la violencia o la fuerza en lugar de en el consentimiento claro de la víctima. Esto dificulta que las víctimas obtengan justicia y apoyo.

**Qué pasó** – En marzo de 2026, los comités del Parlamento Europeo de Libertades Civiles y Derechos de las Mujeres votaron para pedir a la Comisión Europea que cree una ley que defina la violación como un acto sin consentimiento libre, informado y reversible. La votación fue de 75 a favor, 27 en contra y 3 abstenciones. Esta nueva ley reemplazaría las normas antiguas basadas en la violencia.

**Impacto** – Definir la violación por la ausencia de consentimiento es importante porque se centra en la elección de la víctima, no solo en la fuerza física. Se espera que este cambio ayude a que más víctimas denuncien, mejore las decisiones judiciales y proporcione mejores servicios de apoyo como atención médica y asesoramiento.

**Próximo paso** – El informe se presentará al pleno del Parlamento Europeo a finales de marzo de 2026 para su aprobación. Si se aprueba, se instará a la Comisión Europea a proponer la nueva ley. Esto podría llevar a que todos los países de la UE actualicen sus leyes de violación según esta definición común basada en el consentimiento.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo avanza hacia una definición clara de violación basada en el consentimiento en toda la UE para mejorar la justicia y el apoyo a las víctimas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a diverse group of stylized, faceless silhouettes standing united in a circle, their hands gently joined to form an unbroken chain symbolizing consent and solidarity, set against a soft background of interlocking puzzle pieces representing the EU member states harmonizing their laws, with subtle scales of justice balanced above them to convey fairness and protection.

articles.title: Central African Republic enters ‘decisive’ phase after landmark elections
articles.summary: Fresh from holding the most extensive elections in its history, the Central African Republic (CAR) is entering what the UN’s top envoy in the country describes as a “decisive period” in consolidating fragile peace gains, even as security challenges persist and disarmament efforts continue.
articles.content:
Briefing the Security Council on Wednesday, Special Representative Valentine Rugwabiza said the 28 December 2025 polls marked “an important milestone in the consolidation of the peace process and State authority.”

The combined presidential, legislative, regional and municipal elections – including the first municipal polls since 1988 – represented “the most extensive electoral operations ever undertaken in the Central African Republic,” she said.

Voting was conducted “in a peaceful and secure manner across most of the country, despite isolated security incidents.”

Incumbent President Faustin-Archange Touadéra secured an outright majority and won a third term in office.

UN support for the elections

The UN peacekeeping mission, MINUSCA, provided technical, logistical, operational and security support, including transporting more than 232 tonnes of voter information and facilitating the deployment of nearly 500 election-related workers nationwide.

The mission also supported the rotation and reinforcement of 1,237 national defence and security forces and provided assistance packages to more than 800 soldiers in hotspot areas.

The elections also reflected progress on inclusion, with women representing more than 47 per cent of voters and 45 per cent of municipal candidates.

Special Representative Rugwabiza briefing the Security Council via video link.

Implementing the peace accord

CAR has faced recurrent cycles of conflict since 2013, when predominantly Muslim Seleka rebels seized power, prompting the rise of largely Christian anti-Balaka militias.

The 2019 Political Agreement for Peace and Reconciliation between the Government and 14 armed groups – brokered with international support – remains the central framework for stabilisation efforts.

Ms. Rugwabiza reported continued progress under that agreement, including the dissolution of two additional armed groups and the signing of a third agreement for the return of the Movement of Central African Patriots to the process, facilitated by Chad.

Since July 2025, more than 1,200 combatants have been disarmed and demobilised.

Security improved, but challenges remain

However, she cautioned that “the continuation of a well-planned and coordinated disarmament and demobilisation process remains essential to sustain security and peace gains.”

Security conditions have improved in western and central regions, but challenges remain acute in the northeast and southeast.

In the southeast, militia attacks targeting civilians and local authorities continue, after 21 voting centres out of 6,700 nationwide were prevented from opening there on election day.

The spillover of conflict from neighbouring Sudan has driven thousands of refugees into northeastern prefectures, straining limited services and exacerbating intercommunal tensions – while further constraining humanitarian access.

© MINUSCA MINUSCA peacekeepers on patrol in a market town in Central African Republic in the lead up to the December 2025 elections.

Cuts to peacekeeping

MINUSCA has consolidated its force presence in affected areas to enhance civilian protection. At the same time, the mission’s operations have been impacted by a lack of money.

As of 2 February, $733 million of the mission’s $1.17 billion annual budget from July 2025-June 2026 remains unpaid (approximately 63 per cent).

Established in 2014, MINUSCA is mandated to protect civilians, support the peace process and help extend State authority.

As the country enters a post-election phase, the mission is seeking to gradually transfer responsibilities to national institutions – a process dependent on continued stability, institutional capacity and reliable financial resources.

Building peace

Omar Hilale, Morocco’s Ambassador to the UN and Chair of the Peacebuilding Commission’s support group working to bolster peace in CAR, said the country was entering “a decisive phase in terms of consolidation, confidence and national cohesion.”

The UN Peacebuilding Commission is an intergovernmental advisory body supporting peace efforts in conflict-impacted nations worldwide.

“We must ensure that political attention to security sector reform remains unwavering,” said Mr. Hilale, while also underscoring the importance of MINUSCA. Any discussions on its future must be anchored in the “realities on the ground,” he said.

“The Central African Republic is entering a new phase, turning resolutely towards the future with renewed determination and commitment. It is, therefore, incumbent upon us to seize this pivotal moment, and consolidate and safeguard the progress made so far,” he said.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the Central African Republic's successful holding of its most extensive elections ever, marking a significant milestone in consolidating peace and state authority after years of conflict. The peaceful conduct of elections, inclusive voter participation, disarmament of combatants, and ongoing peacebuilding efforts represent meaningful progress with broad societal impact. Despite ongoing security challenges, the story focuses on tangible advances in peace, governance, and national cohesion, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Central African Republic holds historic elections, marking new steps toward peace
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Central African Republic (CAR) has faced years of conflict and instability since 2013, with armed groups fighting for control. In 2019, a peace agreement was signed to help end the violence and bring stability.

**What happened** – On December 28, 2025, CAR held its largest elections ever, including presidential, legislative, regional, and municipal votes. These elections were peaceful in most areas, with President Faustin-Archange Touadéra winning a third term. The United Nations helped by providing security, transporting election materials, and supporting thousands of workers nationwide.

**Impact** – These elections are an important step in building peace and strengthening the government’s authority. More than 47% of voters and 45% of municipal candidates were women, showing progress in inclusion. Over 1,200 fighters have been disarmed since July 2025, making communities safer. Despite some violence in certain regions and challenges from refugees fleeing nearby conflicts, the country is moving forward.

**What's next step** – The UN peacekeeping mission MINUSCA will keep supporting security and help transfer responsibilities to national authorities. Continued disarmament and peacebuilding efforts are needed, along with steady funding, to keep improving safety and stability.

**One-sentence takeaway** – CAR’s peaceful and inclusive elections mark a key moment in its journey toward lasting peace and stronger government control.
articles.personality_title_fr: La République centrafricaine organise des élections historiques, franchissant une nouvelle étape vers la paix
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La République centrafricaine (RCA) connaît des années de conflits et d’instabilité depuis 2013, avec des groupes armés en lutte pour le pouvoir. Un accord de paix a été signé en 2019 pour mettre fin aux violences et apporter la stabilité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 28 décembre 2025, la RCA a organisé ses plus grandes élections jamais réalisées, incluant présidentielles, législatives, régionales et municipales. Ces élections se sont déroulées pacifiquement dans la plupart des régions, avec la réélection du président Faustin-Archange Touadéra pour un troisième mandat. L’ONU a aidé en assurant la sécurité, en transportant le matériel électoral et en soutenant des milliers de travailleurs.

**Impact** – Ces élections sont une étape importante pour construire la paix et renforcer l’autorité du gouvernement. Plus de 47 % des votants et 45 % des candidats municipaux étaient des femmes, montrant des progrès en matière d’inclusion. Plus de 1 200 combattants ont été désarmés depuis juillet 2025, améliorant la sécurité. Malgré des violences dans certaines régions et des défis liés aux réfugiés, le pays avance.

**Prochaine étape** – La mission de maintien de la paix de l’ONU, MINUSCA, continuera à soutenir la sécurité et à aider au transfert des responsabilités aux autorités nationales. Les efforts de désarmement et de consolidation de la paix doivent se poursuivre avec un financement stable pour améliorer la stabilité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les élections pacifiques et inclusives de la RCA marquent un moment clé dans son chemin vers une paix durable et un gouvernement plus fort.
articles.personality_title_es: República Centroafricana celebra elecciones históricas, dando nuevos pasos hacia la paz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La República Centroafricana (RCA) ha vivido años de conflicto e inestabilidad desde 2013, con grupos armados luchando por el control. En 2019 se firmó un acuerdo de paz para poner fin a la violencia y traer estabilidad.

**Qué pasó** – El 28 de diciembre de 2025, la RCA realizó sus elecciones más grandes hasta la fecha, que incluyeron presidenciales, legislativas, regionales y municipales. Las elecciones fueron pacíficas en la mayoría del país, y el presidente Faustin-Archange Touadéra ganó un tercer mandato. La ONU ayudó con seguridad, transporte de materiales electorales y apoyo a miles de trabajadores.

**Impacto** – Estas elecciones son un paso importante para construir la paz y fortalecer la autoridad del gobierno. Más del 47 % de los votantes y el 45 % de los candidatos municipales fueron mujeres, mostrando progreso en inclusión. Más de 1,200 combatientes han sido desarmados desde julio de 2025, mejorando la seguridad. A pesar de violencia en algunas áreas y retos por refugiados, el país avanza.

**Próximo paso** – La misión de paz de la ONU, MINUSCA, seguirá apoyando la seguridad y ayudando a transferir responsabilidades a las autoridades nacionales. Se necesitan continuar los esfuerzos de desarme y construcción de paz con fondos constantes para mejorar la estabilidad.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las elecciones pacíficas e inclusivas de la RCA marcan un momento clave en su camino hacia una paz duradera y un gobierno más fuerte.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vibrant, peaceful Central African village square filled with colorful ballot boxes and silhouettes of diverse hands—male and female—casting votes, surrounded by symbolic blue peacekeeper helmets and gentle doves soaring above, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones.

articles.title: This coastal idyll banned 'harmful' holiday lets. Eight years on, has it worked?
articles.summary: A ban on new holiday homes sees more permanent residents but some say the policy harms tourism.
articles.content: Nearly eight years ago, Beadnell on the Northumberland coast became one of the first villages in the country to ban any homes built on new sites being used as second homes or holiday lets. The number of permanent residents has increased, bringing what some say is "new life" but others argue the policy is not enough to save rural communities or think it an unwarranted attack on tourism.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a policy banning new holiday homes to prioritize permanent residents, which positively impacts community sustainability and housing availability in a rural area. This has meaningful social benefits by supporting local populations and addressing housing challenges, though it also includes some debate about tourism impact.
articles.personality_title: Beadnell’s ban on new holiday homes boosts local community after eight years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nearly eight years ago, the village of Beadnell on England’s Northumberland coast decided to stop new homes from being used as holiday lets or second homes. This was one of the first places in the country to try this approach.

**What happened** – Since the ban started, no new houses built in Beadnell can be sold or rented only to tourists or part-time owners. Instead, these homes must be for people who live there all year round. This change helped increase the number of permanent residents in the village.

**Impact** – More people living in Beadnell all year has brought new energy to the community. It also means there are more homes available for local families rather than just holiday visitors. Some people feel this helps keep the village alive and supports local shops and services. However, others worry it might reduce the number of tourists who visit and spend money.

**What’s next step** – The village and local leaders will watch how the policy continues to affect both residents and tourism. They may consider adjusting the rules if needed to balance community life with visitors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Beadnell’s ban on new holiday homes has increased permanent residents and helped local community life while sparking debate about tourism’s role.
articles.personality_title_fr: Beadnell interdit les nouvelles résidences secondaires et renforce sa communauté après huit ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a près de huit ans, le village de Beadnell, sur la côte de Northumberland en Angleterre, a décidé d’interdire que les nouvelles maisons soient utilisées comme résidences secondaires ou locations de vacances. C’était l’une des premières zones à appliquer cette règle.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Depuis cette interdiction, aucune nouvelle maison construite à Beadnell ne peut être vendue ou louée uniquement à des touristes ou des propriétaires à temps partiel. Ces maisons doivent être habitées toute l’année. Cette mesure a fait augmenter le nombre de résidents permanents.

**Impact** – Plus de personnes vivant toute l’année à Beadnell a apporté une nouvelle énergie à la communauté. Cela signifie aussi que plus de logements sont disponibles pour les familles locales plutôt que pour les visiteurs de passage. Certains pensent que cela aide à maintenir la vie du village et soutient les commerces locaux. Mais d’autres craignent que cela réduise le nombre de touristes.

**Prochaine étape** – Le village et les responsables locaux surveilleront comment cette politique influence les habitants et le tourisme. Ils pourraient ajuster les règles pour trouver un bon équilibre.

**Phrase clé** – L’interdiction des nouvelles résidences secondaires à Beadnell a augmenté les résidents permanents et renforcé la vie locale, tout en posant des questions sur le tourisme.
articles.personality_title_es: Beadnell prohíbe nuevas casas de vacaciones y fortalece su comunidad tras ocho años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace casi ocho años, el pueblo de Beadnell, en la costa de Northumberland en Inglaterra, decidió prohibir que las casas nuevas se usaran como segundas residencias o alquileres vacacionales. Fue uno de los primeros lugares en aplicar esta medida.

**Qué pasó** – Desde la prohibición, ninguna casa nueva en Beadnell puede venderse o alquilarse solo a turistas o propietarios temporales. Estas viviendas deben ser para personas que vivan todo el año. Esto aumentó el número de residentes permanentes.

**Impacto** – Más personas viviendo todo el año en Beadnell ha traído nueva vida a la comunidad. También significa que hay más casas disponibles para familias locales en lugar de solo visitantes. Algunas personas creen que esto ayuda a mantener el pueblo y apoya a las tiendas locales. Pero otros temen que pueda reducir el turismo.

**Próximo paso** – El pueblo y los líderes locales seguirán observando cómo afecta esta política a los residentes y al turismo. Podrían cambiar las reglas para equilibrar la vida comunitaria y las visitas.

**Frase clave** – La prohibición de nuevas casas de vacaciones en Beadnell ha aumentado los residentes permanentes y fortalecido la comunidad local, mientras genera debate sobre el turismo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a peaceful Northumberland coastal village with cozy, modest cottages nestled along the shore, some with small gardens and smoke gently rising from chimneys, symbolizing permanent homes; in the foreground, a calm sea with a single traditional fishing boat resting quietly, representing the enduring local community and balanced tourism, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted blues.
